walkthrough.title: Determine the Start Player
walkthrough.text: Let's get Roll Player set up. First, everyone rolls a die. Whoever rolls highest is the Start Player. They will kick off the game and handle a few housekeeping duties each round. Put all 73 dice into the cloth dice bag. There are 10 dice each in green, blue, red, purple, black, and white, plus 13 gold dice. Do not sort them. Just dump them all in and mix them up.

walkthrough.title: Choose Character Sheets
walkthrough.text: Starting with the Start Player and going clockwise, each player picks a Character Sheet. There are six different sheets, each featuring a unique Race like Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Orc, or Dragonkin. Each sheet has a male side and a female side. Pick whichever side you like — it is purely cosmetic and does not affect gameplay. Your Race does matter though, because some Races have bonuses or penalties applied to specific Attribute Rows during final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Gold
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 5 Gold from the supply. If there is a third player, that third player in turn order gets 1 extra Gold, so 6 total. If there is a fourth player, the fourth player gets 2 extra Gold, so 7 total. This compensates for going later in the first round. Put the rest of the Gold coins in a central supply everyone can reach.

walkthrough.title: Draw Class Cards
walkthrough.text: Starting with the Start Player and going clockwise, each player draws a random die from the dice bag and takes the Class card matching that die's color. For example, if you draw a white die, you take the white Class card. Each Class card is double-sided with two Class options. Pick one side. If you draw a gold die or a color that matches another player's Class, keep drawing until you get a unique color. Gold dice drawn during this step do not give you bonus Gold. Once everyone has a Class, return all drawn dice to the bag. Alternatively, your group can skip the random draw and instead let players choose Class cards in reverse turn order.

walkthrough.title: Deal Backstory, Alignment, and Player Aid Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Backstory cards into their own deck and the Alignment cards into their own deck. Deal one Backstory card and one Alignment card face down to each player. Also give each player a Player Aid card. The Player Aid has a handy turn summary on the front and a scoring chart on the back. Return any unused Backstory, Alignment, and Player Aid cards to the box.

walkthrough.title: Place Tracking Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player takes the two tracking tokens that match their Class color. Place one token on the center space of your Alignment card. This token will shift around as you buy Trait and Skill cards during the game. Place the other token on your Class card. You will use this during final scoring to tally your Reputation Stars.

walkthrough.title: Build the Market Deck
walkthrough.text: Separate the 53 Market cards into two piles based on the dots in the upper right corner. Single-dot cards are simpler and appear early. Double-dot cards are more powerful and appear later. For 3 players, randomly discard 3 cards from each pile. For 2 players, discard 7 from each pile. Now shuffle each pile separately, then stack the single-dot pile on top of the double-dot pile to form the Market deck. This ensures simpler cards come out first.

walkthrough.title: Create the Market
walkthrough.text: Draw cards from the top of the Market deck equal to the number of players plus one. So that is 3 cards for 2 players, 4 cards for 3 players, or 5 cards for 4 players. Lay them face up in a row in the center of the table. These are the Market cards available for purchase this round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Initiative Cards
walkthrough.text: Lay out the Initiative cards in a row, numbered in order. The number of Initiative cards in play equals the number of Market cards. For 4 players, use cards 1 through 5. For 3 players, use 1 through 4 and return the 5 card to the box. For 2 players, use 1 through 3 and return the 4 and 5 cards.

walkthrough.title: Place Gold on Initiative Cards
walkthrough.text: Now place 1 Gold on each Initiative card that is neither first nor last in numerical order. For 2 players, place 1 Gold on the number 2 card only. For 3 players, place Gold on the 2 and 3 cards. For 4 players, place Gold on the 2, 3, and 4 cards. The first and last cards never get Gold. These coins are an incentive for players who take the middle Initiative cards.

walkthrough.title: Draw Starting Dice
walkthrough.text: Starting with the Start Player, each player draws their starting dice from the bag without looking inside. The number of dice equals the number of players plus four. So 6 dice for 2 players, 7 for 3 players, or 8 for 4 players. Keep your dice hidden until everyone has drawn.

walkthrough.title: Roll and Place Starting Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player rolls all their starting dice and then arranges them on their Character Sheet. Dice must be placed in the leftmost empty space of any Attribute Row. You cannot skip spaces. Important: you do not take Attribute Actions during setup. However, for each Attribute Row you completely fill with 3 dice, gain 1 Gold from the supply. And for each gold die you placed, gain 2 Gold. Exception: if you have the Thief Class, you gain 4 Gold per gold die instead of 2. Take your time placing these dice. Think about your Class card's Attribute Goals and your Backstory card's color requirements.

walkthrough.title: Begin the Game
walkthrough.text: Setup is done. The Start Player begins the first round. You are now ready to draft dice, buy market cards, and build the greatest adventurer who ever lived. Let's learn how a round works.

summary.title: Determine the Start Player
bullets: Everyone rolls a die — highest roll is Start Player
Place all 73 dice (6 colors x 10 + 13 gold) into the dice bag

summary.title: Choose Character Sheets
bullets: Starting with Start Player, clockwise, each player picks a Character Sheet
Choose male or female side (cosmetic only)
Each sheet features a unique Race with potential Attribute bonuses/penalties

summary.title: Distribute Starting Gold
bullets: Each player takes 5 Gold
3rd player in turn order gets +1 Gold; 4th player gets +2 Gold
Remaining Gold forms the central supply

summary.title: Draw Class Cards
bullets: Each player draws a die from the bag and takes the matching-color Class card
Choose one of two Class options on the double-sided card
If gold or duplicate color drawn, redraw until unique
Alternative: choose Classes in reverse turn order

summary.title: Deal Backstory, Alignment, and Player Aid Cards
bullets: Shuffle Backstory and Alignment decks separately
Deal 1 Backstory + 1 Alignment + 1 Player Aid to each player
Return unused cards to the box

summary.title: Place Tracking Tokens
bullets: Take 2 tracking tokens of your Class color
Place 1 on center of Alignment card, 1 on Class card
Alignment token moves during play; Class token tracks final scoring

summary.title: Build the Market Deck
bullets: Separate Market cards by dot count (single-dot vs. double-dot)
Discard 3 from each pile (3 players) or 7 from each (2 players)
Shuffle piles separately, stack single-dot on top of double-dot

summary.title: Create the Market
bullets: Draw cards equal to players + 1 (3/4/5 for 2/3/4 players)
Place face up in a row — these are available for purchase

summary.title: Set Up Initiative Cards
bullets: Lay out Initiative cards in numerical order
Number of cards = number of Market cards (players + 1)
Remove card 5 for 3 players; remove 4 and 5 for 2 players

summary.title: Place Gold on Initiative Cards
bullets: Place 1 Gold on each middle Initiative card (not first, not last)
2 players: Gold on card 2 only
3 players: Gold on cards 2 and 3
4 players: Gold on cards 2, 3, and 4

summary.title: Draw Starting Dice
bullets: Each player draws dice equal to players + 4 (6/7/8 for 2/3/4 players)
Draw from bag without looking inside

summary.title: Roll and Place Starting Dice
bullets: Roll all starting dice and place on Character Sheet
Must fill leftmost empty space in any Attribute Row
No Attribute Actions during setup
Gain 1 Gold per completed row (3 dice); gain 2 Gold per gold die (Thief: 4 Gold)

summary.title: Begin the Game
bullets: Start Player begins the first round
Game is played over a series of rounds until all Attribute Rows are filled

walkthrough.title: Overview of a Round
walkthrough.text: Roll Player is played over a series of rounds. The game ends when all players have completely filled every Attribute Row on their Character Sheets, which means 18 dice total per player. Each round has four phases: Roll, Dice, Market, and Cleanup. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: The Start Player draws dice from the bag equal to the number of Initiative cards in play. That is 3 dice for 2 players, 4 for 3 players, or 5 for 4 players. Roll them all. These dice form the Dice Pool for the round. Now place them on the Initiative cards in order from lowest to highest value. The lowest die goes on the number 1 card, the next lowest on the number 2 card, and so on. If two or more dice show the same value, the Start Player decides which goes where.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase — Select an Initiative Card
walkthrough.text: Starting with the Start Player and going clockwise, each player picks one Initiative card along with its die and any Gold sitting on it. Place the Initiative card in front of your Character Sheet. This is a key decision. Low-numbered cards let you buy from the Market first, but higher-numbered cards often have better dice values and Gold on them.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase — Place the Die
walkthrough.text: Take the die from your chosen Initiative card and place it in the leftmost empty space of any Attribute Row on your Character Sheet. You cannot skip spaces within a row. Think carefully about which row to place it in. Consider your Class card's Attribute Goals, your Backstory card's color requirements, and which Attribute Action you want to take.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase — Collect Gold
walkthrough.text: After placing your die, collect any Gold you are owed. There are three possible sources this turn. First, take any Gold that was on your Initiative card. Second, if you placed a gold die, gain 2 Gold from the supply. Third, if the die you placed fills the third and final space in an Attribute Row, gain 1 Gold. All three can happen on the same turn.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase — Attribute Actions
walkthrough.text: After placing your die, you may take the Attribute Action for the row where you placed it. This is optional but often very powerful. There are six different actions, one per Attribute Row. You can only take one Attribute Action per turn, and it must correspond to the row where you just placed a die. You can use the action on any die on your sheet, not just the one you placed this turn. Dice moved or changed by an Attribute Action do not trigger additional actions.

walkthrough.title: Strength Action — Flip a Die
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Strength row, you may flip any one die on your Character Sheet to its opposite face. Remember, opposite sides of a die always add up to 7. So a 1 becomes a 6, a 2 becomes a 5, and a 3 becomes a 4. This is extremely useful for hitting exact Attribute Goal totals.

walkthrough.title: Dexterity Action — Swap Two Dice
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Dexterity row, you may swap the positions of any two dice on your Character Sheet without changing their face values. This helps you rearrange colors for your Backstory card or move high or low values to rows where they help meet Attribute Goals. You cannot move a die to an empty space using this action.

walkthrough.title: Constitution Action — Adjust a Die by One
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Constitution row, you may increase or decrease the face value of any one die on your Character Sheet by exactly 1. Turn a 3 into a 4 or a 4 into a 3. Important: values do not wrap around. A 6 cannot become a 1, and a 1 cannot become a 6. This gives you fine-tuned control over your Attribute totals.

walkthrough.title: Intelligence Action — Reroll a Die
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Intelligence row, you may pick up any one die on your Character Sheet and reroll it. After rolling, you choose whether to keep the new value or the old value. Either way, the die goes back to its original space. This is a gamble, but it can pay off when you need a specific value and have nothing to lose.

walkthrough.title: Wisdom Action — Shift Alignment
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Wisdom row, you may move your tracking token on the Alignment card one space in any direction: up, down, left, or right. Your Alignment card position affects final scoring, so strategically nudging it toward a favorable position is valuable.

walkthrough.title: Charisma Action — Take a Charisma Token
walkthrough.text: When you place a die in the Charisma row, you may take a Charisma token. During the Market Phase this round, you can spend a Charisma token in place of 1 Gold when buying a Market card. Unused Charisma tokens are discarded at the end of the round, so use it or lose it.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase — Buying Cards
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken their dice, one Initiative card remains unclaimed in the center. Now the Market Phase begins. Players buy cards in order of their Initiative card numbers, lowest first. To buy a card, pay its Gold cost shown in the upper right corner. Place the card in the matching area on your Character Sheet: Weapons, Armor, Traits, or Skills. If you buy a Trait card, immediately move your Alignment tracking token as indicated by the arrow on the card. If you buy a Skill card, it is immediately available to use.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase — Passing on a Buy
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or choose not to buy a Market card, you must pick one card from the Market and add it to the discard pile. You then gain 2 Gold from the supply. This ensures the Market refreshes and gives you something even when nothing appeals to you. After buying or discarding, return your Initiative card to the row in the center.

walkthrough.title: Weapon Card Rules
walkthrough.text: Weapon cards have a hand icon showing one or two hands. Your character can never have more than two hands worth of Weapons. That means you can have two one-handed weapons, one two-handed weapon, or one one-handed weapon with a free hand. If you want to buy a new Weapon that would put you over the limit, you must first discard an existing Weapon to the Market discard pile. Some Weapons modify Attribute Scores during final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Armor, Trait, and Skill Card Basics
walkthrough.text: Armor cards have no in-game effect but score Reputation Stars for collecting sets of the same type: Chain, Leather, or Mystic. There is no limit to how many Armor cards you can have. Trait cards adjust your Alignment when purchased and score Reputation Stars if you meet their end-game criteria. You can have as many as you want. Skill cards give you special abilities you can activate during the game. When you use a Skill, you must first move your Alignment token as shown on the card, then you may use the ability, then you exhaust the card by turning it sideways. An exhausted Skill cannot be used again until refreshed.

walkthrough.title: Using Skill Cards
walkthrough.text: Skill cards deserve extra attention because they have unique timing rules. A Skill can be used at any time, even during another player's turn, unless the card says otherwise. To use a Skill, you must be able to move your Alignment token in the direction shown on the card. If your token is already at the edge and cannot move that direction, you cannot use the Skill. After moving Alignment, resolve the Skill's ability, then exhaust it by turning it sideways. You can only refresh one exhausted Skill per round, and that happens during the Cleanup Phase.

walkthrough.title: Cleanup Phase
walkthrough.text: After the Market Phase, clean up for the next round. First, each player discards any unused Charisma tokens. Second, each player may refresh one exhausted Skill card by turning it upright. Third, the Start Player returns the remaining die from the unclaimed Initiative card to the dice bag and discards any remaining Market cards. Fourth, draw new Market cards equal to the number of Initiative cards and lay them out. If the Market deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new deck. Fifth, place 1 Gold on any middle Initiative cards that do not already have Gold. Initiative cards never hold more than 1 Gold. Sixth, pass the dice bag to the player on the left. That player is the new Start Player.

walkthrough.title: Four Ways to Gain Gold
walkthrough.text: Gold is your currency for buying Market cards, so keep track of it. There are four standard ways to gain Gold. First, gain 2 Gold when you place a gold die on your Character Sheet. Second, gain 2 Gold when you discard a Market card instead of buying one. Third, gain 1 Gold when you fill the third space in an Attribute Row. Fourth, gain 1 Gold when you select an Initiative card that has Gold on it. Some Skill, Weapon, and Class abilities also grant Gold.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends at the completion of the round in which all players have filled every Attribute Row on their Character Sheet. That means all 18 spaces are occupied with dice. Finish out the full round including the Market and Cleanup Phases, then move to Final Scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring — Attribute Goals
walkthrough.text: Now for scoring. Each player uses their Class card tracking token and the chart on the back of their Player Aid to tally Reputation Stars. First up: Attribute Goals. For each Attribute Row, add the face values of all three dice, then adjust for any Race bonuses or penalties on your Character Sheet and any Weapon card modifiers. Compare the adjusted total to the Attribute Goal on your Class card. If the goal shows a single number like 17, you must hit it exactly. If it shows a range like 16-17, you must be within that range. If it shows a number with a plus sign like 14+, your score must be equal or higher. Earn the listed Reputation Stars for each goal you meet.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring — Other Categories
walkthrough.text: Next, count Class Color Dice. Each die on your sheet matching your Class color is worth 1 Reputation Star. Then check your Alignment card. The tracking token's position shows how many Reputation Stars you gain or lose. Then score your Backstory card: compare dice colors and positions to the card. 0-1 matches equals 0 Stars, 2-3 matches equals 1 Star, 4-5 matches equals 3 Stars, and all 6 matches equals 6 Stars. Then score Armor sets: count cards in each Armor type (Chain, Leather, Mystic), match the count to the Stars shown on those cards, plus earn a bonus Star per set if your Class color matches. Finally, add up Reputation Stars from any Trait cards whose conditions you met.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The player with the most Reputation Stars wins. If tied, the player with the most Gold wins. If still tied, the player with the fewest dice in their Class color wins. And if still tied, the tied players share the victory. Congratulations to the greatest hero ever created!

summary.title: Overview of a Round
bullets: Game plays over multiple rounds until all 18 Attribute spaces are filled
Each round has 4 phases: Roll, Dice, Market, Cleanup

summary.title: Roll Phase
bullets: Start Player draws dice from bag equal to number of Initiative cards
Roll all drawn dice to form the Dice Pool
Place on Initiative cards lowest to highest (Start Player breaks ties)

summary.title: Dice Phase — Select an Initiative Card
bullets: Starting with Start Player, clockwise, each player picks an Initiative card
Take the die and any Gold on the card
Low numbers = buy first in Market; high numbers = better dice/Gold

summary.title: Dice Phase — Place the Die
bullets: Place die in leftmost empty space of any Attribute Row
Cannot skip spaces within a row
Consider Class Goals, Backstory colors, and desired Attribute Action

summary.title: Dice Phase — Collect Gold
bullets: Take Gold from Initiative card (if any)
Gain 2 Gold for placing a gold die
Gain 1 Gold for filling the 3rd space in an Attribute Row

summary.title: Dice Phase — Attribute Actions
bullets: Optional action matching the row where you placed the die
Only 1 Attribute Action per turn
Can target any die on sheet, not just the one placed this turn
Moved/changed dice do not trigger further actions

summary.title: Strength Action — Flip a Die
bullets: Flip any die to its opposite face (1<->6, 2<->5, 3<->4)
Opposite sides always sum to 7

summary.title: Dexterity Action — Swap Two Dice
bullets: Swap positions of any 2 dice, keeping face values unchanged
Cannot move a die to an empty space

summary.title: Constitution Action — Adjust a Die by One
bullets: Increase or decrease any die's value by 1
No wrapping: 6 cannot become 1, 1 cannot become 6

summary.title: Intelligence Action — Reroll a Die
bullets: Pick up any die and reroll it
Choose to keep new value or original value
Die returns to its original space

summary.title: Wisdom Action — Shift Alignment
bullets: Move Alignment tracking token 1 space: up, down, left, or right
Nudge toward a favorable scoring position

summary.title: Charisma Action — Take a Charisma Token
bullets: Take 1 Charisma token
Spend as 1 Gold when buying a Market card this round
Unused Charisma tokens discarded at end of round

summary.title: Market Phase — Buying Cards
bullets: Buy order: lowest Initiative card number goes first
Pay Gold cost to buy a Weapon, Armor, Trait, or Skill card
Trait cards: immediately move Alignment token as shown
Skill cards: immediately available for use

summary.title: Market Phase — Passing on a Buy
bullets: If you don't buy, discard 1 Market card and gain 2 Gold
Return your Initiative card to the center row
Anyone can look through the Market discard pile at any time

summary.title: Weapon Card Rules
bullets: Weapons show 1-hand or 2-hand icons
Max 2 hands worth of Weapons at a time
Discard a Weapon to make room for a new one
Some Weapons modify Attribute Scores in final scoring

summary.title: Armor, Trait, and Skill Card Basics
bullets: Armor: no in-game effect; score for sets of same type (Chain/Leather/Mystic)
Traits: shift Alignment on purchase; score if end-game criteria met
Skills: special abilities; exhaust after use; refresh 1 per round
No limit on Armor, Trait, or Skill card count

summary.title: Using Skill Cards
bullets: Usable at any time (even other players' turns) unless card says otherwise
Must move Alignment token in indicated direction to use — if blocked, cannot use
Exhaust after use (turn sideways); refresh 1 Skill per round in Cleanup
Only upright Skills can be used

summary.title: Cleanup Phase
bullets: Discard unused Charisma tokens; refresh 1 exhausted Skill card
Return leftover die to bag; discard remaining Market cards
Draw new Market cards; replenish Gold on middle Initiative cards
Pass dice bag left — new Start Player

summary.title: Four Ways to Gain Gold
bullets: Place a gold die: +2 Gold
Discard a Market card instead of buying: +2 Gold
Fill 3rd space in an Attribute Row: +1 Gold
Select Initiative card with Gold on it: +1 Gold

summary.title: Game End Trigger
bullets: Game ends when all players have filled all 18 Attribute spaces
Complete the full round (including Market and Cleanup), then score

summary.title: Final Scoring — Attribute Goals
bullets: Sum dice in each row + Race bonus/penalty + Weapon modifiers
Compare to Class card goals: exact number, range, or minimum (14+)
Earn listed Reputation Stars for each goal met

summary.title: Final Scoring — Other Categories
bullets: Class Color Dice: 1 Star per die matching Class color
Alignment: gain or lose Stars per token position
Backstory: 0-1 matches=0, 2-3=1, 4-5=3, 6=6 Stars
Armor sets: Stars per set size + bonus Star if set matches Class color
Traits: Stars from Trait cards whose criteria are met

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Most Reputation Stars wins
Tiebreak 1: most Gold
Tiebreak 2: fewest Class-color dice
Tiebreak 3: shared victory

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a scripted first round with three players: Alex, Beth, and Carlos. Alex is the Start Player. Each player has already completed setup — they have their Character Sheet, Class card, Backstory card, Alignment card, starting dice placed, and some Gold. We will follow each phase of the round so you can see how everything fits together. Let's go.

walkthrough.title: The Players' Setup
walkthrough.text: Alex is playing a Human Ranger (green Class) and needs high Dexterity and Wisdom for his Attribute Goals. Beth is playing an Elf Wizard (purple Class) and needs high Intelligence. Carlos is playing a Dwarf Fighter (red Class) and wants high Strength and Constitution. Each player has around 5-7 Gold and their starting dice already placed on their Character Sheets.

walkthrough.title: Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: Alex is the Start Player, so he draws 4 dice from the bag (3 players means 4 Initiative cards). He rolls them and gets: a blue 2, a red 5, a gold 3, and a green 6. Now he places them on the Initiative cards from lowest to highest. The blue 2 goes on card 1, the gold 3 goes on card 2, the red 5 goes on card 3, and the green 6 goes on card 4. Remember, cards 2 and 3 each have 1 Gold on them from setup.

walkthrough.title: Alex Picks an Initiative Card
walkthrough.text: Alex goes first. He eyes the green 6 on card 4 — it matches his Ranger Class color and is a high value for his Dexterity goal. But card 4 has no Gold on it and he would buy last in the Market. Instead, Alex takes card 2 with the gold 3 die. He collects the 1 Gold sitting on the card. A gold die plus Gold on the card — nice income round for Alex.

walkthrough.title: Alex Places His Die and Takes Action
walkthrough.text: Alex places the gold 3 in the first empty space of his Wisdom row. He gains 2 Gold for placing a gold die. Now he can take the Wisdom Attribute Action, which lets him move his Alignment token one space. He checks his Alignment card and nudges the token one space toward Lawful Good, which is worth more Reputation Stars on his particular card. Solid turn.

walkthrough.title: Beth Picks an Initiative Card
walkthrough.text: Beth goes next. She takes card 3 with the red 5 and collects the 1 Gold on it. The red die does not match her purple Wizard Class color, but the 5 is a good value for her Intelligence row where she needs a high total.

walkthrough.title: Beth's Mistake — Ignoring Backstory Colors
walkthrough.text: Beth places the red 5 in her Intelligence row. She takes the Intelligence Attribute Action and rerolls a purple 2 already on her sheet, hoping for a higher value. She rolls a 4 — nice improvement. But here is the problem. Beth's Backstory card shows a purple die in that exact Intelligence position. By rerolling and keeping the 4, the die is still purple, so the color matches. However, she just placed a red die in a space where her Backstory wanted a purple die. She did not even look at her Backstory card before deciding where to place the red 5. That is a missed opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Your Backstory Card
walkthrough.text: Your Backstory card is easy to forget because its payoff only comes at game end. But matching 4-5 dice positions earns you 3 Reputation Stars, and a perfect 6 matches earns a whopping 6 Stars. Before you place every die, glance at your Backstory card and ask: does this color belong in this position? Sometimes placing a slightly worse die in the right spot is worth more than chasing a perfect Attribute total.

walkthrough.title: Carlos Picks the Last Initiative Card
walkthrough.text: Carlos has two cards left: card 1 with the blue 2 and card 4 with the green 6. He takes card 4 for the green 6. He wants high Strength, and a 6 is exactly what he needs. Card 1 with the blue 2 remains unclaimed in the center — its die will go back to the bag during Cleanup.

walkthrough.title: Carlos Places His Die
walkthrough.text: Carlos places the green 6 in the last open space of his Strength row, completing it with three dice. He gains 1 Gold for filling the row. He takes the Strength Attribute Action and flips a black 1 in his Constitution row to a 6. Nice — that is a 5-point swing for his Constitution total. Carlos is sitting pretty.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: Now it is Market time. There are 4 Market cards face up. Players buy in Initiative order: Alex had card 2 so he goes first, Beth had card 3 so she is second, and Carlos had card 4 so he goes last. Let's see what they do.

walkthrough.title: Alex Buys a Skill Card
walkthrough.text: Alex sees a Skill card that costs 3 Gold. He has 8 Gold after this round's income, so he can afford it. He pays 3 Gold and places the Skill card next to his Character Sheet. It is immediately available for use. The Skill moves his Alignment one space when activated, which he will need to plan around. Alex returns his Initiative card to the center.

walkthrough.title: Beth's Mistake — Buying Armor She Does Not Need
walkthrough.text: Beth spots a Chain Armor card for 2 Gold. It is cheap, so she buys it on impulse. But her Wizard is purple Class, and this Chain Armor does not match purple. She now has one piece of Chain Armor, which by itself scores only 1 Reputation Star. A single Armor card without building toward a set is almost always a waste of Gold. She would have been better off saving her Gold for a Skill or Trait card that aligned with her strategy, or discarding a card for 2 Gold to build her savings for next round.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Buy Armor Only if You Are Committing to Sets
walkthrough.text: Armor cards do nothing during the game. They only score at the end, and they score on a curve: more cards of the same type earns significantly more Stars. One Chain Armor is worth only 1 Star. Two Chain is worth 3. Three is worth 5. Four is worth 7. If you are not planning to buy multiple pieces of the same Armor type, that Gold is better spent elsewhere. Also, if your Class color matches the Armor type, you get a bonus Star per set, making it even more valuable to commit fully.

walkthrough.title: Carlos Buys a Trait Card
walkthrough.text: Carlos is last to buy but there are still good options. He picks up a Trait card for 4 Gold. It immediately moves his Alignment token one space as indicated by the card's arrow. The Trait card will earn him 2 Reputation Stars at game end if his Strength Attribute Score is 15 or higher, which he is on track for. Smart purchase that aligns with his strategy.

walkthrough.title: Beth's Mistake — Forgetting to Use Her Skill
walkthrough.text: During Carlos's Market Phase turn, Beth suddenly realizes she has a Skill card from a previous round that she forgot to use the entire round. Skills can be used at any time, even on other players' turns. She could have used it during the Dice Phase to manipulate her dice, but she forgot it was there. The Skill sits unused, and she can only refresh one Skill per round during Cleanup anyway. This is a wasted opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Your Skills Visible and Active
walkthrough.text: Skill cards are powerful but easy to forget. Place them prominently beside your Character Sheet where you can see them. At the start of each phase, glance at your Skills and ask whether now is a good time to activate one. Remember, you can use Skills during any phase and even on other players' turns. The Alignment cost means you should plan when to use them, but never let a round pass with an upright Skill you meant to activate.

walkthrough.title: Cleanup Phase
walkthrough.text: Now we clean up. Alex has no Charisma tokens to discard. Beth refreshes her forgotten Skill card — well, it was already upright since she never used it. Carlos has nothing to refresh. The Start Player (Alex) returns the unclaimed blue 2 die to the bag and discards the remaining Market card. He draws 4 new Market cards for next round. Gold is checked on the Initiative cards — cards 2 and 3 already had Gold taken this round, so 1 Gold is placed on each of them. Finally, Alex passes the dice bag to Beth. She becomes the new Start Player for Round 2.

walkthrough.title: Round Summary
walkthrough.text: In one round, you saw all four phases in action. The Roll Phase sets the table with dice on Initiative cards. The Dice Phase is where the biggest decisions happen: which card to take, where to place the die, and which Attribute Action to use. The Market Phase lets you enhance your character with equipment and abilities. And the Cleanup Phase resets everything for the next round. Each round follows the same pattern until all Attribute Rows are filled.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through a complete round of Roll Player. You saw Alex draft strategically for Gold income and Alignment positioning. You watched Beth make two common mistakes — ignoring her Backstory card and buying Armor without a set strategy — and you saw Carlos flip a die for a huge value swing. You are ready to play on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Roll
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common pitfalls to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick-reference guide to every Attribute Action, Market card type, and scoring category.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun creating heroes!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players: Alex (Ranger), Beth (Wizard), Carlos (Fighter)
Covers a complete first round through all 4 phases
Follow along even if your group has different characters

summary.title: The Players' Setup
bullets: Alex: Human Ranger (green), needs Dexterity and Wisdom
Beth: Elf Wizard (purple), needs Intelligence
Carlos: Dwarf Fighter (red), needs Strength and Constitution

summary.title: Roll Phase
bullets: Start Player draws 4 dice (3 players), rolls, places lowest to highest on Initiative cards
Cards 2 and 3 have Gold on them from setup

summary.title: Alex Picks an Initiative Card
bullets: Weighs high-value green die vs. Gold income + buy order
Takes card 2 with gold 3 die and 1 Gold on the card

summary.title: Alex Places His Die and Takes Action
bullets: Places gold 3 in Wisdom row, gains 2 Gold for gold die
Uses Wisdom Action to shift Alignment toward favorable position

summary.title: Beth Picks an Initiative Card
bullets: Takes card 3 with red 5 and 1 Gold
Red doesn't match her purple Class, but 5 is good for Intelligence total

summary.title: Beth's Mistake — Ignoring Backstory Colors
bullets: Places red die in a spot where Backstory wanted a purple die
Did not check Backstory card before choosing placement
Missed opportunity for end-game Backstory scoring

summary.title: Lesson: Always Check Your Backstory Card
bullets: Backstory matching is worth up to 6 Reputation Stars
Check color requirements before every die placement
Sometimes a slightly worse die value in the right color spot is worth more

summary.title: Carlos Picks the Last Initiative Card
bullets: Takes card 4 with green 6 for his Strength row
Card 1 with blue 2 remains unclaimed

summary.title: Carlos Places His Die
bullets: Completes Strength row — gains 1 Gold
Uses Strength Action to flip a 1 to a 6 in Constitution (+5 swing)

summary.title: Market Phase Begins
bullets: 4 face-up Market cards available
Buy order: Alex (card 2), Beth (card 3), Carlos (card 4)

summary.title: Alex Buys a Skill Card
bullets: Pays 3 Gold for a Skill card
Immediately available for use this round and beyond

summary.title: Beth's Mistake — Buying Armor She Does Not Need
bullets: Buys 1 Chain Armor on impulse — only 1 Star for a lone piece
Not building toward a set, and it doesn't match her Class color
Gold would have been better saved or spent on a Skill or Trait

summary.title: Lesson: Buy Armor Only if You Are Committing to Sets
bullets: Armor scores on a curve: 1=1, 2=3, 3=5, 4=7 Stars
Single Armor pieces are low value; commit to a full set or skip
Class color match gives a bonus Star per set

summary.title: Carlos Buys a Trait Card
bullets: Buys Trait for 4 Gold — moves Alignment immediately
Trait awards 2 Stars if Strength reaches 15+, which he's on track for

summary.title: Beth's Mistake — Forgetting to Use Her Skill
bullets: Had an upright Skill card all round but never activated it
Skills can be used at any time, even on other players' turns
Wasted a round of potential benefit

summary.title: Lesson: Keep Your Skills Visible and Active
bullets: Place Skill cards prominently where you can see them
Check your Skills at the start of every phase
Plan your Alignment movement budget around Skill usage

summary.title: Cleanup Phase
bullets: Discard Charisma tokens, refresh 1 exhausted Skill
Return unclaimed die to bag, discard leftover Market cards
Draw 4 new Market cards, replenish Gold on middle Initiative cards
Pass dice bag left — Beth becomes new Start Player

summary.title: Round Summary
bullets: Roll Phase sets the dice, Dice Phase is the big decision point
Market Phase enhances your character, Cleanup resets for next round
Same pattern repeats until all Attribute Rows are full

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You practiced all 4 phases of a round
Saw 3 common mistakes: ignoring Backstory, impulse Armor buys, forgetting Skills
You're ready to play on your own — go create a legendary hero!

summary.title: Before You Roll
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls
Reference the Appendix for Attribute Actions, card types, and scoring rules
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Plan Around Your Class Card First
walkthrough.text: Your Class card is the single most important scoring factor. It tells you exactly what Attribute totals you need for each row, and each goal can be worth multiple Reputation Stars. Before you place any die, check your Class card. If your Ranger needs Dexterity at 14+, every die placement should consider whether it helps or hurts that target. Build your entire strategy around meeting as many Attribute Goals as possible, because those Stars are reliable and high-value.

walkthrough.title: Check Your Backstory Card Every Turn
walkthrough.text: New players consistently forget about their Backstory card, and it costs them. The Backstory shows six specific die colors in specific Attribute Row positions. Matching all six is worth a massive 6 Reputation Stars. Even matching 4-5 gets you 3 Stars. Before every die placement, glance at your Backstory card and ask: does this die color belong in this spot? Sometimes the best play is to place a die in a row that is not your first choice for the Attribute Action but matches your Backstory perfectly.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Gold in the Early Rounds
walkthrough.text: Gold is the only way to buy Market cards, and Market cards are what separate a good character from a great one. In the early rounds, do not be afraid to take Initiative cards with Gold on them even if the die is not ideal. Taking the Initiative card with 1 Gold on it plus placing a gold die gives you 3 Gold in one turn. Early Gold lets you buy the best Skill and Trait cards before other players can afford them. Once you have a solid card collection, you can shift focus to pure die optimization.

walkthrough.title: Use Attribute Actions Aggressively
walkthrough.text: The Attribute Actions are what make Roll Player more than just a lucky dice game. Strength lets you flip a die for up to a 5-point swing. Constitution gives you fine-tuned control with plus or minus 1. Dexterity lets you swap dice to nail Backstory positions. Do not play passively. Every time you place a die, think about which Attribute Action gives you the most benefit right now. Sometimes placing a die in a row where you do not really need it is worth it just to access a powerful action.

walkthrough.title: Balance Initiative Card Selection
walkthrough.text: Choosing your Initiative card is the trickiest decision each round. Low-numbered cards give you first pick in the Market Phase, which is huge when a key card is available. But high-numbered cards have better dice values, and middle cards come with Gold. Think about what you need most this round. If the Market has a must-buy card, grab a low Initiative. If you need a high die for an Attribute Goal, go high. If you need Gold, aim for the middle. There is no single right answer — it depends on the round.

walkthrough.title: Commit to an Armor Strategy or Skip It
walkthrough.text: Armor cards score on a steep curve. One piece of Chain Armor is just 1 Star. Two is 3 Stars. Three is 5. Four is 7. A single random Armor buy is almost never worth it. Either commit to buying multiple pieces of the same type across several rounds, or do not buy Armor at all. If your Class color matches the Armor type, you get a bonus Star per set, which makes committing even more rewarding. Check the Armor available early and decide whether it is part of your plan.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Alignment Carefully
walkthrough.text: Your Alignment token shifts every time you buy a Trait card, use a Skill card, or take the Wisdom Attribute Action. It is easy to lose track of where your token is heading. Some Alignment positions are worth positive Reputation Stars, others are worth negative Stars. Before buying a Trait or using a Skill, check where the Alignment shift will move your token. If it pushes you into a negative zone, the card might not be worth it even if its other benefits look good. Use the Wisdom Action to course-correct when needed.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect the Discard Option
walkthrough.text: New players feel like they must buy something every Market Phase. They should not. Discarding a card from the Market and gaining 2 Gold is a perfectly valid and often smart play. If nothing in the Market fits your strategy, if you are saving for an expensive card next round, or if the remaining Market cards are all bad for you, take the 2 Gold. It is better to save your money than to waste 4-5 Gold on a card that does not contribute to your scoring plan.

summary.title: Plan Around Your Class Card First
bullets: Class card Attribute Goals are the most reliable source of Reputation Stars
Check goals before every die placement
Build your entire strategy around meeting as many goals as possible

summary.title: Check Your Backstory Card Every Turn
bullets: Backstory matching: 6 matches = 6 Stars, 4-5 matches = 3 Stars
Glance at Backstory before every die placement
Sometimes placing in a suboptimal row is worth it for color matching

summary.title: Prioritize Gold in the Early Rounds
bullets: Gold lets you buy key Skill and Trait cards before opponents
Initiative cards with Gold + gold dice = big income turns
Build Gold early, optimize dice late

summary.title: Use Attribute Actions Aggressively
bullets: Strength flip = up to 5-point swing; Constitution = fine-tuned +/-1
Dexterity swaps fix Backstory positions; Intelligence rerolls are worth the gamble
Sometimes place a die in a row just for its Attribute Action

summary.title: Balance Initiative Card Selection
bullets: Low number = buy first in Market (grab must-buy cards)
High number = better dice values for Attribute Goals
Middle cards = Gold income
Evaluate what you need most this round

summary.title: Commit to an Armor Strategy or Skip It
bullets: Armor scoring curve: 1=1, 2=3, 3=5, 4=7 Stars
Single Armor buys are almost never worth it
Class color match gives bonus Star per set
Decide early whether Armor is part of your plan

summary.title: Watch Your Alignment Carefully
bullets: Trait buys, Skill uses, and Wisdom Actions all shift Alignment
Some positions are positive Stars, others negative
Check Alignment impact before buying Traits or using Skills
Use Wisdom Action to course-correct

summary.title: Do Not Neglect the Discard Option
bullets: Discarding a Market card for 2 Gold is a valid and often smart play
Do not waste Gold on cards that don't fit your strategy
Save Gold for high-impact purchases in future rounds

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Attribute Goal Stars
walkthrough.text: Attribute Goals are typically the largest single source of Reputation Stars. Advanced players plan their entire die placement sequence around hitting every goal on their Class card. The key insight is to work backward from the target totals. If your Class needs Strength at exactly 17, and you have a 5 and a 6 already placed, you need a 6 in the last slot. Use Attribute Actions to sculpt your dice: Strength flips, Constitution adjustments, and Intelligence rerolls can all fine-tune your totals. Prioritize rows with the highest Star rewards first.

walkthrough.title: Backstory Optimization
walkthrough.text: Matching all 6 Backstory positions earns 6 Reputation Stars — one of the highest single payoffs in the game. Advanced players treat Backstory as a die-color drafting guide. When choosing Initiative cards in the Dice Phase, factor in die color alongside value. A green 3 that matches your Backstory may be worth more than a blue 6 that does not. Use Dexterity swaps to rearrange dice colors into their correct Backstory positions after placement. In the mid-game, count your current matches and calculate whether pursuing the next Backstory tier is realistic.

walkthrough.title: Alignment End-Game Planning
walkthrough.text: Your Alignment card can swing your score by several Stars in either direction. Advanced players plan their Alignment trajectory from the first Trait purchase. Map out which Traits and Skills you plan to buy and count the total directional shifts. If buying three Trait cards would push you into a negative zone, maybe buy only two. The Wisdom Attribute Action is your safety valve — use it to steer back toward a high-scoring position. The ideal end state is the corner or edge position with the maximum positive Stars on your specific Alignment card.

walkthrough.title: The Gold Economy
walkthrough.text: Gold management separates good players from great ones. Each round you have limited opportunities to gain Gold, and Market card prices range from 1 to 8. Advanced players track their Gold income versus planned purchases several rounds ahead. Taking the 2 Gold discard option in an early round to afford a critical Skill card in the next round is often the winning play. Gold dice are doubly valuable because they provide Gold income and can fill any Backstory slot as a wild-ish option. The Thief Class, which earns 4 Gold per gold die, has a massive economic advantage if you lean into it.

walkthrough.title: Skill Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Skills are the most complex cards in Roll Player because they can be used at any time, even on opponents' turns. Advanced players think about Skill timing in terms of Alignment budget. Each Skill use costs an Alignment shift, so you need to plan how many times you can afford to use Skills without wrecking your Alignment position. Some Skills are best used in the Dice Phase to manipulate just-placed dice. Others are best saved for the Market Phase or even triggered reactively on opponents' turns. Remember, you can only refresh one Skill per round, so if you have multiple Skills, plan which one to refresh based on next round's needs.

walkthrough.title: Armor Set Commitment
walkthrough.text: The Armor scoring curve rewards deep commitment. A full set of 5 Chain Armor scores 10 Stars by itself, plus a bonus Star if your Class color matches. That is an enormous payoff, but it requires buying 5 Armor cards over the course of the game — potentially spending 10-15 Gold on cards with no in-game effect. The advanced play is to identify early whether Armor is available in sufficient quantity and whether your Class color matches a type. If you see 2-3 of the same Armor type in the early Market, commit immediately. If not, skip Armor entirely and spend that Gold on Traits and Skills.

walkthrough.title: Class Ability Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Each Class has a unique ability that bends the normal rules. Advanced players build their entire game around exploiting their Class ability to its fullest. The Bard (Charming) can buy two Market cards per round, enabling aggressive set collection. The Druid (Attuned) can pull discarded cards back into the Market, effectively giving you access to cards opponents thought were gone. The Thief gains extra Gold from gold dice, funding an aggressive buying strategy. Study your Class ability during setup and plan how to use it every single round.

walkthrough.title: Denying Opponents
walkthrough.text: Roll Player is not purely a solo puzzle. Advanced players watch what opponents are building and make denial plays when the cost is low. If an opponent is collecting Chain Armor and a Chain card appears in the Market, consider buying or discarding it to break their set. When choosing where to place tied dice during the Roll Phase as Start Player, put the better die on a higher-numbered Initiative card so the last player to pick gets a worse option. Denying a key card costs you nothing if you were going to discard from the Market anyway.

walkthrough.title: Dice Color Drafting
walkthrough.text: Every die in your Class color is worth 1 Reputation Star. With 18 dice on your sheet, even 4-5 matching dice adds up to significant Stars for free. Advanced players factor Class color into every Initiative card selection. When two dice are equally useful for Attribute Goals, always take the one that matches your Class color. This also synergizes with Backstory scoring — if your Class color appears frequently on your Backstory card, you get double benefit from drafting that color. Gold dice are neutral for Class color but provide Gold income, making them a solid fallback.

walkthrough.title: Trait Card Evaluation
walkthrough.text: Trait cards are deceptively powerful because they offer Reputation Stars with no in-game ability cost. But each Trait shifts your Alignment when purchased, which can be a hidden cost. Advanced players evaluate Traits on three axes: how many Stars does it offer, how likely am I to meet its end-game condition, and where does the Alignment shift take me? A 3-Star Trait that pushes your Alignment into a negative zone might net you only 1 Star after the Alignment penalty. Conversely, a 1-Star Trait that moves your Alignment toward a positive position is worth more than it appears. Always calculate the net effect before buying.

summary.title: Maximizing Attribute Goal Stars
bullets: Work backward from target totals to plan die placements
Use Strength flips, Constitution adjustments, Intelligence rerolls to sculpt totals
Prioritize rows with highest Star rewards
Attribute Goals are usually the biggest single scoring source

summary.title: Backstory Optimization
bullets: 6 matches = 6 Stars — treat Backstory as a color-drafting guide
Factor die color into Initiative card selection alongside value
Use Dexterity swaps to rearrange colors into Backstory positions
Count current matches and calculate if next tier is realistic

summary.title: Alignment End-Game Planning
bullets: Map total Alignment shifts from planned Trait/Skill purchases
Avoid buying cards that push you into negative Alignment zones
Wisdom Action is your safety valve — use it to steer back
Target the highest-scoring corner or edge position

summary.title: The Gold Economy
bullets: Track Gold income vs. planned purchases multiple rounds ahead
Taking 2 Gold discard now to afford a critical Skill next round is often correct
Gold dice: income + flexible Backstory wild color
Thief Class has massive economic advantage — lean into gold dice

summary.title: Skill Card Timing
bullets: Each Skill use costs an Alignment shift — budget your shifts carefully
Use during Dice Phase to manipulate dice, or save for Market Phase
Only 1 Skill refreshes per round — plan which one to refresh
Some Skills work reactively on opponents' turns

summary.title: Armor Set Commitment
bullets: 5 Chain Armor = 10 Stars + potential Class color bonus
Requires 10-15 Gold investment with no in-game effect
Commit early if 2-3 of same type appear, otherwise skip entirely
Class color match makes commitment even more rewarding

summary.title: Class Ability Exploitation
bullets: Bard: 2 Market buys per round enables aggressive set collection
Druid: pull discards back into Market for targeted shopping
Thief: extra Gold from gold dice funds aggressive buying
Plan your entire game around maximizing your Class ability

summary.title: Denying Opponents
bullets: Watch opponents' Armor sets and deny key pieces
As Start Player, place tied dice to disadvantage later pickers
Denial is free when you're discarding from Market anyway
Awareness of opponents' Class Goals helps you draft defensively

summary.title: Dice Color Drafting
bullets: Each Class-color die = 1 free Reputation Star
When two dice are equally useful, always take Class color
Synergizes with Backstory if Class color appears on Backstory card
Gold dice are neutral for color but provide Gold income

summary.title: Trait Card Evaluation
bullets: Evaluate: Stars offered, likelihood of meeting condition, Alignment shift impact
A 3-Star Trait pushing Alignment negative may net only 1 Star
A 1-Star Trait improving Alignment position may be worth more than it looks
Always calculate net effect (Trait Stars + Alignment change) before buying

entries.definition: One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: flip any die on your Character Sheet to its opposite face (opposite sides sum to 7). A 1 becomes 6, 2 becomes 5, 3 becomes 4.
One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: swap the positions of any two dice on your Character Sheet without changing their face values. You cannot move a die to an empty space.
One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: increase or decrease the face value of any die on your Character Sheet by 1. Values do not wrap (6 cannot become 1, 1 cannot become 6).
One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: reroll any die on your Character Sheet. Choose to keep the new value or the original. The die returns to its original space.
One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: move the tracking token on your Alignment card one space in any direction (up, down, left, or right).
One of the six Attribute Rows. Attribute Action: take a Charisma token. During the Market Phase this round, spend it in place of 1 Gold. Unused Charisma tokens are discarded during Cleanup.
The sum of the face values of all three dice in an Attribute Row, adjusted by any Race bonuses/penalties and Weapon card modifiers. Compared to Class card goals during Final Scoring.

An optional action taken after placing a die in an Attribute Row. Each row has a unique action. Only one can be taken per turn, and it can target any die on the Character Sheet, not just the one placed this turn.
A target value for an Attribute Row shown on a player's Class card. May be an exact number (e.g., 17), a range (e.g., 16-17), or a minimum (e.g., 14+). Meeting a goal earns Reputation Stars.
The victory points in Roll Player. Earned during Final Scoring from Attribute Goals, Class Color Dice, Alignment, Backstory, Armor sets, and Trait cards. Most Stars wins.
During Final Scoring, each die on your Character Sheet that matches your Class card color earns 1 Reputation Star. A reliable passive scoring source.
Match dice colors on your Character Sheet to the colors and positions shown on your Backstory card. 0-1 matches = 0 Stars, 2-3 = 1 Star, 4-5 = 3 Stars, 6 = 6 Stars.
At game end, your tracking token position on the Alignment card determines Stars gained or lost. Grid positions range from positive to negative values. Plan shifts carefully.
Sort Armor cards by type (Chain, Leather, Mystic). Each set scores Stars based on count (shown on the cards). Earn 1 bonus Star per set if the Armor matches your Class color.
Each player's board showing their Race, six Attribute Rows with 3 spaces each (18 total), and areas for Class, Backstory, Alignment, and Market cards. Each sheet features a unique Race.
Contains all 73 dice. Draws are always made without looking. If too many dice are drawn, return them all and redraw. The bag is passed left each round to the new Start Player.
There are 7 die colors: green, blue, red, purple, black, white (10 each), and gold (13). Each non-gold color corresponds to a Class card color.
A special die color. Placing a gold die on your Character Sheet grants 2 Gold (4 Gold for Thief Class). Gold dice do not match any Class color for Class Color Dice scoring.
Numbered cards (1-5) that organize gameplay. Dice are placed on them during Roll Phase. Players select them during Dice Phase. The number determines Market Phase buy order (lower = earlier).

Each player gets 2 tokens in their Class color. One tracks position on the Alignment card (shifts during play). The other sits on the Class card and is used for tallying Reputation Stars during Final Scoring.
Gained from the Charisma Attribute Action. Can be spent as 1 Gold during the Market Phase this round. Unused tokens are discarded during the Cleanup Phase.
Reference card for each player. Front shows the sequence of play. Back shows the scoring chart for tallying Reputation Stars during Final Scoring.

A Market card type. Provides ongoing abilities or Attribute Score bonuses. Shows a 1-hand or 2-hand icon. Players may never have more than 2 hands worth of Weapons. Discard an existing Weapon to make room for a new one.

A Market card type. No in-game effect. Scores Reputation Stars for collecting sets of the same type: Chain, Leather, or Mystic. More cards in a set = more Stars. Class color match = bonus Star per set.
A Market card type. When purchased, immediately move the Alignment tracking token as shown by the arrow icon. At game end, earn Reputation Stars if you meet the card's criteria.

A Market card type. Provides a special ability usable at any time (even other players' turns), unless the card says otherwise. To use: move Alignment, resolve ability, exhaust (turn sideways). Refresh 1 Skill per round in Cleanup.
One of three Armor types. Collect sets for Reputation Stars. Set scoring: 1=1, 2=3, 3=5, 4=7, 5=10 Stars. Bonus Star if your Class color matches.
One of three Armor types. Collect sets for Reputation Stars. Set scoring follows the same curve as Chain. Leather sets max at 4 cards. Bonus Star if Class color matches.
One of three Armor types. Collect sets for Reputation Stars. Mystic sets max at 3 cards. Bonus Star if Class color matches.

The draw pile for Market cards. Built during setup with single-dot cards on top of double-dot cards. Cards drawn during Cleanup to refresh the Market each round. If depleted, shuffle the discard pile to form a new deck.

Market cards have a dot indicator in the upper right corner. Single-dot cards are simpler and cheaper, appearing in the first half of the game. Double-dot cards are more powerful and expensive, appearing later.

Found on Trait and Skill cards. Indicates the direction to move the Alignment tracking token when the card is purchased (Traits) or used (Skills). If movement is impossible due to token position, the Skill cannot be used.
When a Skill card is used, it is turned sideways (exhausted). An exhausted Skill cannot be used again until refreshed. Only 1 Skill may be refreshed per round, during the Cleanup Phase.
Turning an exhausted (sideways) Skill card upright so it can be used again. Each player may refresh only 1 Skill per round, during the Cleanup Phase.

Defines a character's adventuring profession. Double-sided with two Class options. Shows Attribute Goals, a Class-specific ability, and the Class color. Each Class color matches one of the six die colors.
Red Class. Focused on high Strength and Constitution. Class ability varies by card side.
Green Class. Often focused on Dexterity and Wisdom. Class ability varies by card side.
Purple Class. Focused on high Intelligence. Class ability varies by card side.
White Class card. Two Class options: Cleric focuses on Wisdom and Charisma; Paladin has different Attribute Goals. Class ability varies by chosen side.
A Class option that gains 4 Gold (instead of 2) for each gold die placed on the Character Sheet. Massive economic advantage when gold dice are drafted frequently.

A Class with the Charming ability. May buy two Market cards in a single Market Phase. Both buys happen during the player's turn. Charisma token applies to only one buy. Cannot discard cards for Gold using Charming.
A Class with the Attuned ability. May return any card from the Market discard pile to the Market before the Market Phase begins. Adds to (does not replace) existing Market cards.

Tracks a character's moral and ethical position on a 3x3 grid. Ranges from Lawful to Chaotic (horizontal) and Good to Evil (vertical). Token starts in the center. Shifted by Trait purchases, Skill uses, and the Wisdom Attribute Action.

The 3x3 grid on the Alignment card showing nine possible positions. Each position has a Reputation Star value (positive, zero, or negative). Token movement is limited to one space at a time: up, down, left, or right.
Shows an 18-space grid (6 rows x 3 columns) with 6 specific dice colors in specific positions. At game end, match dice on your Character Sheet to the Backstory card for Reputation Stars.
Each Character Sheet features a unique Race (e.g., Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Orc, Dragonkin). Some Races apply bonuses or penalties to specific Attribute Row totals during Final Scoring.
A modifier printed on the Character Sheet that adds to or subtracts from a specific Attribute Row's total during Final Scoring. Applied after summing dice values and before comparing to Class goals.
The player who draws and rolls dice during the Roll Phase. Picks first during the Dice Phase. Handles Cleanup duties. The role passes to the left each round via the dice bag.
The set of dice drawn and rolled during the Roll Phase. Placed on Initiative cards from lowest to highest value. One die remains unclaimed after the Dice Phase and returns to the bag during Cleanup.
First phase of each round. Start Player draws dice equal to the number of Initiative cards, rolls them, and places them on Initiative cards from lowest to highest value.
Second phase. Starting with Start Player, clockwise, each player selects an Initiative card, places the die, collects Gold, and optionally takes an Attribute Action.
Third phase. Players buy Market cards (or discard for Gold) in Initiative card number order (lowest first). One card per player per round (except Bard's Charming ability).
Fourth and final phase. Discard Charisma tokens, refresh 1 Skill, return leftover die to bag, discard remaining Market cards, draw new Market, replenish Initiative Gold, pass Start Player left.
A Weapon card. If the player has no Armor or only complete Armor sets (5 Chain, 4 Leather, or 3 Mystic), no additional Reputation Stars are earned from Tower Shield.
Weapon cards that can make dice count as greater than 6 when scoring Attribute Goals. No effect on Trait card requirements (e.g., Reckless, Weak).
A Weapon card that lets you pay 1 fewer Gold when buying Market cards. Works with Move Silently when buying from the discard pile.
A Skill card. When used, Diplomacy is exhausted while the targeted Skill card is not. You can use Diplomacy to adjust your Alignment without taking the target Skill's action.
Skill cards that affect dice on unselected Initiative cards during the Dice Phase. When reordering dice, follow the Roll Phase ordering rules as if you were the Start Player.
A Skill card. Lets you buy a card from the Market discard pile. Cannot also discard a Market card for 2 Gold in the same turn. Charisma tokens may be used. Works with Heavy Crossbow discount.
A Skill card. If you place a gold die from the Dice Pool on your sheet, you still gain 2 Gold. When reordering Initiative card dice, follow Roll Phase ordering rules as Start Player.
A Skill card. Lets you buy a card from the top of the Market deck. Discounts (Charisma tokens, Heavy Crossbow) cannot be used. If the card is a Trait, you must move your Alignment token.
A Skill card. Lets you look inside the dice bag and select a specific die. If a gold die is selected and placed, you gain the normal 2 Gold.
A Skill card. Moving a die does not trigger the Attribute Action of the new row, and Gold is not gained if the die moves to the 3rd space. After using, slide remaining dice left to close gaps.
